songs.title: R-R-Rat!
songs.lyrics:
[Intro]
R! R! R-r-r!
(rrr… rrr… rrr…)

[Hook]
R! R! R-r-r!
What begins with R?
R-r-rabbit! R-r-rabbit!
It's a rabbit! (a rabbit!)

[Verse 2]
R! R! R-r-r!
What begins with R?
R-r-rocket! R-r-rocket!
It's a rocket! (a rocket!)

[Verse 3]
R! R! R-r-r!
What begins with R?
R-r-ring! R-r-ring!
It's a ring! (a ring!)

[Verse 4]
R! R! R-r-r!
What begins with R?
R-r-robot! R-r-robot!
It's a robot! (a robot!)

[Bridge]
A potato?! (NO!)
P-p-potato! Not R!
(rrr… rrr… rrr…)

[Outro]
R-r-rat! The red rat!
R-r-rat! (rip, rip, rip!)
R! R! R-r-r!
songs.sunoStyle: dark trap, 68 bpm, heavy 808 bass, sparse hi-hats, deep whisper-rap verses, kids choir chant on hook, playful spooky, minimal, clean vocals, nursery trap
songs.sunoNotes: 🚨 /r/ is the HARDEST sound for Mandarin-L1 children — there is no English /r/ in Mandarin and they reach for /l/ or /w/. Sing it as a long curled American 'rrr' with the tongue tip pulled back and NOT touching anything (never a trilled or tapped r, never 'ar' the letter name, never 'wuh' or 'luh'). The '(rrr… rrr…)' drone is a held growl — the class holds it with the singer. This song is a pronunciation drill disguised as a hook; hold every initial r an extra beat.

songs.title: The Red Rat
songs.lyrics:
[Intro]
(rrr… rat… rat…)

[Hook]
The red rat! The red rat!
What can the red rat do?
The red rat ran! (it ran!)
The red rat ran, ran, ran!

[Verse 2]
The red rat! The red rat!
What can the red rat do?
The red rat sat! (it sat!)
The red rat sat, sat, sat!

[Verse 3]
The red rat! The red rat!
What can the red rat do?
The red rat hid! (it hid!)
The red rat hid, hid, hid!

[Verse 4]
The cat ran at the rat!
The dog ran at the cat!
Run, rat, run! (run, run, run!)
The red rat ran, ran, ran!

[Outro]
The red rat got a bag…
In the bag… a potato!
(POTATO!)
The red rat ran, ran, ran!
songs.sunoStyle: dark trap, 68 bpm, heavy 808 bass, sparse hi-hats, deep whisper-rap verses, kids choir chant on hook, playful spooky, minimal, clean vocals, nursery trap
songs.sunoNotes: ONE frame: 'The red rat ___!' with a decodable verb swapping (ran, sat, hid). Rat has just joined the cast — this is his debut. The chase in verse 4 stacks the whole cast (cat, dog). 'Run' has the new short u only from Week 16, so it is used only orally here as a shouted chant, never on the printed page. Land the potato once.

spreads.artNote: A sleek little rat, bright-eyed, mid-scurry. Rat joins the cast — introduce him as the quick, clever one.
The same rat, now in a tiny red coat / red-tinted, striking. The reveal of his colour.
The red rat streaking across the frame, motion lines, gleeful.

spreads.text: The red rat got a bag.
spreads.artNote: The rat dragging a brown paper bag almost bigger than himself, determined.
The rat tearing into the bag, paper shreds flying, delighted.

spreads.text: The cat ran at the rat!
spreads.artNote: The Cat bursting into frame after the rat, both mid-run. Cast callback, tension.

spreads.text: The red rat hid in the bag.
spreads.artNote: The rat diving into the bag to hide, only his red tail sticking out.

spreads.text: The cat sat on the bag!
spreads.artNote: The Cat sitting smugly on the lumpy bag, sure it has won. The rat's outline visible inside.
The Cat lifts the bag — out rolls the POTATO (with its glasses) AND the red rat, both blinking. No noun printed — the class SHOUTS it. Rat grins.

sentences: The cat ran at the rat.
extras: 🚨 /r/ pronunciation clinic (Mandarin-L1 focus): mirror work — tongue tip curled back and floating, never touching; contrast r/l with rat/lat (nonsense), ray/lay, red/led, rip/lip; hold the initial r a full beat before the vowel
Moveable alphabet: build r-a-t, r-e-d, r-i-p, r-a-m — then swap the first sound (rat → cat → bat → hat) to feel r take its place in the family
Rat joins the cast — welcome-the-Rat circle; the quick clever one, add his figure beside the Dog
Red hunt: sort classroom objects that are red; label the red ones with r-e-d word cards

teacherFocus: Welcome the Rat — the quick, clever one — AND run the year's hardest pronunciation clinic: /r/ does not exist in Mandarin, so the class will reach for /l/ or /w/. Mirror work: tongue tip curled back and FLOATING, never touching, never trilled, never the letter name 'ar'. Drill r/l minimal pairs (red/led, rip/lip, rat/lat) and hold every initial r an extra beat. Blend r-a-t, r-e-d, r-i-p. The red-rat-and-the-bag chase gives the whole cast a run-around and a big potato payoff.
